weeks.mainStoryHtml:
March Sadness Part II earned the title. The field stayed small, but the action did not. NASA Al struck first by busting both Hiro and Li-Fo in the same blast, Red spent the middle and late phases turning the table into a demolition site, and by the end the whole game felt like one long argument over who was actually driving the chaos.

That answer wound up being NASA Al. Red may have done the heavier public damage, but NASA Al stayed alive long enough to write the ending himself. He survived the swingiest stretch of the night, watched Red clear most of the room, then took out Red to close the whole thing and cash the biggest share of a messy March sequel.

The rebuy count kept the table breathing, the first bust-out came as a double, and the finish landed with the kind of violent little reset that makes this game feel less like a standings update and more like a night somebody will complain about for a week.
weeks.roastHtml: BostnMike managed the deeply on-brand feat of cashing third in a game where Red still personally sent him out late. That is the commissioner's sweet spot: visible, paid, and still somehow part of the problem.
numbersThatMatter: 7 buy-ins, 4 rebuys, 11 total entries.
4h 15m of play from 7:17 pm to 11:32 pm.
$330 total pot with 3 paid spots.
Red led the bloodshed with 5 knockouts, but NASA Al got the final word.
quickHitsLeft: The first bust-out was a two-player splash: Hiro and Li-Fo both hit the rail together.
Providencemike was first out for real, which made Red the first permanent executioner of the night.
BostnMike rebought after NASA Al clipped him early, then still managed to ladder into third.
Li-Fo and Hiro both needed re-entry, which told you early this was not a calm little seven-player sit-down.
quickHitsRight: Red turned the late stretch into a one-man cleanup operation before running into NASA Al at the end.
The session stayed compact but never felt short because the eliminations came in waves.
NASA Al scored the winner line, but this game really had two authors and only one trophy.
March Sadness Part II felt like a sequel in the truest sense: louder, messier, and less interested in subtlety.
weeks.tldr: NASA Al won March Sadness Part II, but Red did most of the public destruction along the way. It was a four-hour game that kept changing shape right up to the final knockout.

weeks.mainStoryHtml:
March Sadness Part I ended up being a comeback story in disguise. Mike-G got busted in the first half of the night, bought back in, and then spent the closing stretch turning the table into his own cleanup operation. That is the sort of line that looks simple in the payout summary and far nastier in the action log.

Kishan helped write the early chapters by busting Mike-G once and later clipping Red, but once the night bent into its final third, it stopped being shared property. Mike-G started cashing everybody out one by one: Nitro, Providencemike, BostnMike, Kishan, and finally Pat to end it. Some wins feel patient. This one felt like a man returning to the scene with receipts.

The rebuys kept the shape of the game loose, Red kept fighting back into the room until Pat finally ended that story, and by the finish the first March Sadness chapter had already set the tone for the sequel: nobody was staying comfortable for long.
weeks.roastHtml: BostnMike managed to land in the exact wrong chapter of the Mike-G redemption arc, which is a very commissioner way to spend an evening: present for the storyline, credited in the summary, and still one of the bodies in the montage.
numbersThatMatter: 7 buy-ins, 4 rebuys, 11 total entries.

$330 total pot with 3 paid spots.
Mike-G finished the night with 5 knockouts after rebuying back into the event.
quickHitsLeft: Providencemike was first out, but not first gone for good.
Red managed three separate bust-outs before Pat finally made him the first permanent casualty.
Mike-G got clipped by Kishan early, then turned around and authored the closing run himself.
This was a very live seven-player field that played bigger because the rebuys kept reopening doors.
quickHitsRight: Pat finished second and also delivered the permanent end to Red's night.
Kishan did real damage before Mike-G finally cut him down in third.
The game stayed almost exactly four hours, which made the late Mike-G takeover feel especially abrupt.
Part I had all the right pre-sequel energy: reversals, re-entries, and one guy stealing the ending.
weeks.tldr: Mike-G lost early, re-entered, and then won March Sadness Part I by wiping out the final stretch himself. It was the cleanest comeback story in the whole seven-game block.

weeks.mainStoryHtml:
Part 2 of the AI Stat-Pack aftermath had the best kind of nonsense: the winner got thrown out first. Ahmed sent Li-Fo to the rail early, then did it again later, and for a long stretch it felt like the whole game belonged to the man collecting receipts. Then the room did what this room does best and made the ending more inconvenient than the middle.

Li-Fo bought back in twice, survived the long grind, and turned the whole thing around at the very end. That is what makes the result memorable. Not just that he won, but that the night kept trying to remove him from the story and he kept climbing back into it anyway. By the time he bust-out BostnMike and then Ahmed to win it, the opener had been completely rewritten.

The other half of the game belonged to Ahmed's body count. He did real damage, especially against Li-Fo and Providencemike, and for most of the session he looked like the cleaner read. But this one belonged to survival more than control, and Li-Fo survived everybody.
weeks.roastHtml: BostnMike rebought, laddered, and still got personally removed by the guy who had already been busted twice. That is the kind of third-place finish that feels less like cashing and more like being named in the postgame lawsuit.

Hiro also needed a reload and still ended up getting removed for good by Ahmed.

quickHitsRight: Cougar was the first player gone for good, which kept the game from becoming completely endless.
BostnMike made the money, then got clipped by Li-Fo right before the finish.
The event rolled past midnight, which matched the long, drawn-out shape of the action summary.
Part 2 was not subtle: it was a reload war with a comeback winner.
weeks.tldr: Li-Fo got busted first, re-entered twice, and still won Part 2 by flipping the ending on Ahmed. It was the messiest comeback in the block and maybe the most fun.

weeks.mainStoryHtml:
Part I belonged to Red in the most TLPT way possible: he got bounced first, bought back in, and then spent the rest of the night making sure everybody remembered he was still there. What followed was not a gentle recovery. It was a one-man demolition job.

Once Red came back, the action summary practically turned into his personal paperwork. Pittsburghbill, Cougar, Ahmed, Nitro, A.I. Dave, and finally Vish all went through him. That is not just a win. That is a post-rebuy rampage, and it turned what could have been a clean little March game into a much nastier opener to the two-part set.

Vish did enough damage to reach heads-up, and Cougar got the original laugh by busting Red first, but neither of those stories survived the late stages. Part I ended up being about the guy who got the first warning and then decided to treat it like motivation.
weeks.roastHtml: BostnMike managed to survive long enough to become part of Vish's highlight reel, which is a very specific kind of late-night insult. Not first out, not final boss, just neatly removed before the end credits.

Cougar got the first laugh by busting Red, then Red erased the joke later himself.
This was a nine-player room that never felt static because the rebuys came at exactly the right times.
quickHitsRight: A.I. Dave quietly hung around and still finished third despite Red bulldozing the back half.
Nitro and Ahmed both got caught in the Red cleanup phase.
The game pushed past four and a half hours, which matched the slow burn before the Red avalanche.
Part I set the tone for the block: rebuys mattered, but closers mattered more.
weeks.tldr: Red got bounced first, came back, and then won Part I by turning the entire late stage into his own knockout reel. It was the loudest rebound in the block.

weeks.mainStoryHtml:
Jesse Pinkman's Makin' Fat Stacks, Yo! did not believe in a clean ending. The field was big enough, the rebuy count was low enough, and the action was spread out just evenly enough that nobody ever really took full custody of the game. That usually makes for a muddled night. Here it made for a weirdly fitting three-way split.

Cougar, NASA Al, and Providencemike all did enough damage to get paid and none of them managed to separate from the other two at the very end. That is the story. Not a dominant closer, not one table killer, just a game that refused to reduce itself to one clean name. Even the knockout race ended in shared custody.

Hiro became the first one gone for good, Vic needed the only rebuy of the night, and BostnMike never saw the podium. But the bigger takeaway was structural: this one stayed balanced long enough that the final result had to be negotiated instead of declared.
weeks.roastHtml: BostnMike found the rare game where the table could not even agree on a single winner and still somehow agreed he should not be part of the payout. That takes a special kind of democratic failure.
numbersThatMatter: 9 buy-ins, 1 rebuy, 10 total entries.

$300 total pot with a three-way split at the top.
Cougar, NASA Al, and Providencemike all finished first.

A.I. Dave drew first blood, but never got near the final shared crown.
This was the cleanest rebuy profile in the block and still one of the strangest finishes.
quickHitsRight: Cougar, NASA Al, and Providencemike also tied the knockout lead, which feels almost rude in its symmetry.
Dan K and Ahmed both disappeared in the final hour as the chop shape started coming into focus.
The night stayed under four and a half hours and still found time for maximum weirdness.
Not every game needs a hero. This one settled for a committee.
weeks.tldr: Jesse Pinkman ended in a three-way chop because nobody quite took complete control. It was balanced, odd, and exactly the kind of finish that makes a weekly recap worth writing.

weeks.mainStoryHtml:
Back from Broadway moved fast and then got mean. Red hit the rail first and still found two rebuys before the room finally got rid of him for good, which tells you most of what you need to know about the early shape of the night. It was busy, loud, and just disorganized enough to leave the door open for someone patient to take over the ending.

That someone was A.I. Dave. Once the table narrowed, he started converting the noise into bodies. Vish, Red, The Architect, BostnMike, and Providencemike all went through him before it was over. That is not just winning. That is a closer showing up for the part of the game where everybody else runs out of ideas.

Providencemike did enough work to reach heads-up, and BostnMike survived a rebuy into third, but the back half of the session belonged to one player and one player only. Broadway came back with a lead actor after all.
weeks.roastHtml: BostnMike rebought, made the money, and still got personally removed by A.I. Dave before the finish. It is always comforting when the commissioner can find a way to both cash and be corrected in the same evening.

weeks.tldr: A.I. Dave took over the late stages and won Back from Broadway with five knockouts and a clean heads-up finish. The early chaos belonged to everybody; the ending belonged to him.

weeks.mainStoryHtml:
The Roast Edition did what the title promised: it made almost everybody look a little ridiculous. Red got bounced first, bought back in, then became the first one gone for good anyway. Ahmed got clipped early by Li-Fo, rebought, and somehow still ended the night sharing first. BostnMike never fully controlled the table and still walked away with a co-win. It was that kind of game.

The weird gravitational center of the night was Li-Fo. He started the damage by busting Red and Ahmed, stayed around long enough to make the finish matter, and then wound up third in a game that never really chose a clean line. That is the sort of result that makes a recap easy: you do not need to exaggerate a night that already behaved like a roast script.

Vic did real work in the middle, BostnMike and Ahmed split the top, and the whole thing closed with a payout summary that looked like somebody had changed channels halfway through the ending. No one was safe. The event title was not kidding.
weeks.roastHtml: BostnMike managed to co-win a game that still refused to make him look fully in charge, which is honestly perfect. Even when the commissioner gets paid, the table likes to leave fingerprints on the result.

quickHitsRight: The final summary gave Ahmed $135 and BostnMike $130, which tells you how oddly the split landed.
Li-Fo made the money while still feeling like the night's main agent of instability.
No One Is Safe ended exactly the way the name suggests: messily and with shared blame.
Some games produce a winner. This one produced an argument and then paid people anyway.
weeks.tldr: The Roast Edition ended in a split first between Ahmed and BostnMike, with Li-Fo finishing third after helping light the fuse early. It was weird, public, and perfectly named.

weeks.mainStoryHtml:
Chip & A Chair, The Tony K Story earned the title the hard way. Vish was first out, rebought, got busted again, rebought again, and still found a way to write the ending himself. It was a long February session that kept handing him exits, and he kept treating them like suggestions.

The table never stayed clean for long. A.I. Dave needed a reload, Providencemike needed a reload, and Vish needed two of them. But once the field narrowed, the night stopped being about rescue missions and started being about momentum. Vish clipped Li-Fo, Nitro, BostnMike, Providencemike, and finally NASA Al to close a comeback that got more absurd with each knockout.

Providencemike did enough early damage to feel like a real threat, and NASA Al stayed alive all the way to heads-up, but the whole story bends around one fact: the first guy out still ended up walking away with the biggest share of the pot.
weeks.roastHtml: BostnMike made it deep enough to matter and still got vacuumed up by the same guy who had already been busted twice. That is the kind of line that looks respectable in the payout math and embarrassing everywhere else.

weeks.mainStoryHtml:
Super Bowl Eve started like a game that might wander all over the place. Nitro hit the rail first and came back, Hiro came back, Providencemike came back, and for a while the table looked like it was going to spend the whole night reopening problems instead of solving them.

Then Providencemike took control of the solving part. Once the field shrank, he started knocking people out in the order that mattered: Cougar, Vish, Hiro, and finally Li-Fo to win it. That sequence gave the game its shape. What began as a rebuy-scrap turned into one player steadily removing the room’s remaining arguments.

Li-Fo made heads-up, Hiro held on for third, and Vish was influential early, but the event is easiest to remember for the way it tightened around one closer at the end.

weeks.mainStoryHtml:
Warning! Obnoxious Pats Fan Onboard! was one of the cleaner January games in structure and one of the stranger ones in finish. There was only one rebuy all night, the field stayed under control, and then the table got all the way to the top without finding a clean single winner.

BostnMike and Wild Bill split first, which fit the action better than a forced solo ending would have. Wild Bill handled Red and Hiro, BostnMike handled A.I. Dave, Hayden D, and Providencemike, and the whole night ended with both finalists busting out together in the official summary. That is not elegant, but it is honest.

Providencemike ran well enough to take third, Hiro opened the bust-out log, and the one-rebuy structure kept the whole thing from turning into a circus. By the end, the Pats-fan warning label felt less like the point than the dead-even finish itself.
weeks.roastHtml: BostnMike got to split first in a game where the table still refused to let him have an uncontested headline. That may be the purest commissioner outcome of all: paid like a winner, framed like a compromise.

Mother, "Maye" I? played like a small game with absolutely no interest in ending on time. Six buy-ins, one rebuy, only two paid spots — and somehow the table still dragged itself through nearly four hours before finally settling for a split at the top.

The funniest part is that Nitro nearly hijacked the story in the middle. He got first blood on Providencemike, then clipped A.I. Dave, and for a stretch the whole game felt like it might become a tiny-field Nitro heist. Instead BostnMike finally ended that run, and the day staggered into a shared finish between Red and the commissioner.

Hayden D was the first one gone for good after his rebuy, Providencemike disappeared early, and the event never once pretended to be in a hurry. It was a small table with a mini-marathon soul.

Ahmed did the early table-cutting, Li-Fo survived long enough to get all the way back to second, and then Cougar simply finished what was left.

That is the real shape of the game. Ahmed busted Li-Fo first and later removed David K and Hiro, which gave him a loud claim on the front half. But once the field tightened, Cougar took over the actual closing duties. Providencemike, BostnMike, Nitro, and Li-Fo all went through him before the event ended.

Li-Fo deserves the weirdest supporting note here: first out, rebought, and still runner-up. That would be the headline in a different game. In this one it became part of Cougar’s winner reel instead.

weeks.mainStoryHtml:
The first game of 2026 opened by dumping Hiro out of the event and closed with Hiro taking the whole thing. That is either a perfect omen for the year or a warning label, depending on how much money you lost watching it happen. Either way, Auld Lang Syne wasted no time turning into a rebuy circus with a comeback finish.

Red did most of the public damage. He busted Hiro first, clipped Vish multiple times, knocked out Chris O, and removed BostnMike before the endgame. For a long stretch it looked like the New Year opener might just be a Red production. Then Hiro came back, steadied himself, and flipped the ending. He busted The Architect and then Red to take it all.

That reversal is the whole story. The game had six rebuys, multiple repeat victims, and enough volume to feel messy from the jump. But the one thing everybody would remember afterward was simple: the first name on the bust-out sheet became the last name standing.

weeks.mainStoryHtml:
Where's the Tylenol?!?! delivered exactly what the title advertised. Chris O got busted first by Red, bought back in, got busted again, bought back in again, and still wound up winning the whole thing. That is not a tidy arc. That is the kind of night that leaves the whole room rubbing its temples on principle.

The event stayed wide open through the middle. The Architect did real damage, Cougar clipped BostnMike, and Red looked like the early author after drawing first blood. Then Chris O came back through the smoke and took the ending. Providencemike, A.I. Dave, The Architect, and Cougar all fell to him in the final stretch, which turned a messy survival story into a pretty clean winner line.

That is what makes this one memorable: the guy who opened as the victim ended as the closer. Two rebuys, four late knockouts, and suddenly the Tylenol was for everyone else.
weeks.roastHtml: BostnMike got taken out by Cougar in the middle of a game that Chris O eventually won after getting busted twice. That is the kind of line that makes a commissioner sound less like a player and more like incidental scenery.

A Holly Jolly kept its holiday shape without ever getting sleepy. The field was modest, the rebuy count stayed manageable, and the game moved in that nice clean way where every knockout feels like it matters. Ahmed and Vic helped make the middle stages noisy, but once the table got short the whole thing tilted toward one closer.

That closer was BostnMike. He handled Vic, then Ahmed, then Chris O to seal the event, which gave the night a much cleaner finish than the early action suggested it might get. Ahmed did real damage in the center of the game, especially teaming on Jeff T and A.I. Dave with Vic, and Chris O survived long enough to make heads-up. But the ending belonged to one player, and he took it personally.

The game never dragged, never spiraled, and never lost its structure. For a holiday-themed event, it was actually pretty businesslike — right up until the commissioner made the last three signatures himself.
weeks.roastHtml: BostnMike won the thing outright, which is annoying enough. But the real insult is how neat the ending looked for him: three late knockouts, first place money, and just enough holiday spirit to make the self-satisfaction unbearable.

Ohhh Fuuuudge played exactly like a December game with five rebuys should. Red was first out and still came back, Li-Fo needed a second life, Providencemike needed two of them, and the whole middle of the event felt like a crowded parking lot with chips. That usually produces nonsense. This time it produced a very clear closer.

Chris O took over once the field got tired of restarting itself. He got Li-Fo once early, then came back for Providencemike again and again, then made Red’s second life the first real permanent casualty, and finally finished Vish, BostnMike, and Li-Fo to end the night. It is one of the cleaner closing runs in this whole archive.

Gilmore had a useful supporting night, BostnMike hung around into third, and Li-Fo survived enough turbulence to make heads-up. But the event title was really about the table, not the winner: everybody else looked like the ones who needed the Tylenol after Chris O was done.
weeks.roastHtml: BostnMike got to cash third in a game where Chris O spent the evening running a full-service correction department. That is a very on-brand commissioner finish: technically profitable, emotionally filed under “fuuuudge.”

$hitter’s Full was a small-field game with absolutely no interest in staying small. Five original buy-ins should make for a quick, quiet night. Instead the room added four rebuys and let Ahmed turn the whole thing into a one-man filing system.

He got Li-Fo first, then Hiro, then Chris O over and over again. By the time the night ended, Ahmed had knocked out Chris O three separate times and still had enough left to take BostnMike and Li-Fo to close the event. That is not just a win in a short field. That is total authorship.

Li-Fo made the best counterargument by rebuying and reaching heads-up, and Hiro still found third after needing a second life. But this game is easiest to remember through repetition: if Ahmed touched your name in the action summary, you probably stayed there.
weeks.roastHtml: BostnMike lasted long enough to cash and still wound up as one more line item in Ahmed’s audit. Third place money is nice. Being reduced to clerical evidence is less so.

Sunday Funday Matinee took all afternoon to decide one thing: whether Li-Fo was actually killable. He was first out, rebought, got busted again, rebought again, then somehow rebought a third time and still reached heads-up. That would have been the whole story in a normal event. This one had another ending in mind.

A.I. Dave stayed upright while the rest of the room kept reopening wounds. I’m Your Huckleberry was not a calm game. It was a marathon with a body count. Yann took over the middle and late-middle stages so completely that the event looked decided long before it actually was. He clipped Hiro twice, wiped out Fabian, caught NASA Al again after the rebuy, then took Li-Fo and A.I. Dave in a row. For a long stretch, this was Yann’s movie.

Then BostnMike hijacked the ending. After drawing first blood on NASA Al way back at the start, he stayed alive through the long grind and finally flipped the whole thing by busting Yann in third and Cougar in second. That is what makes the game memorable. Not just who won, but how much of the night belonged to someone else before the winner suddenly took the final frame.

The runtime was absurd, the action summary looked like two separate movies stitched together, and the end result felt less like a steady climb than a last-minute theft.

weeks.mainStoryHtml:
Gunga Galunga never really opened up into chaos. It stayed tight, mean, and personal. The field was bigger than the opener, the rebuys kept the table alive, and by the time the game reached its late stages it had turned into one of those nights where the winner did not just survive the ending — he authored it.

That winner was BostnMike. What made the game memorable was not simply that he won. It was how he won. Once the final stretch arrived, he started erasing the room himself: Li-Fo out, then Rob F out, then A.I. Dave out to finish the whole thing. Some wins feel inherited. This one felt closed.

The rest of the game gave him plenty to work through. Brad R was first out. Hiro was the first one gone for good. The field kept reloading just enough to make the night feel durable without ever letting it get sloppy. By the end, Gunga Galunga felt like a game with its own center of gravity — and BostnMike had it by the throat.
weeks.roastHtml: BostnMike had the exact kind of night that makes a commissioner unbearable afterward: not just a win, but a closing sequence. There is no worse version of confidence than a guy who can now say, correctly, that he personally took care of the last three problems on the table.
numbersThatMatter: 9 buy-ins, 2 rebuys, 11 total entries.
4h 05m of play from 7:08 pm to 11:13 pm.
$330 total pot with 3 paid spots.
Bostnmike closed the final three knockouts: Li-Fo, Rob F, and A.I. Dave.
quickHitsLeft: Brad R took first-bust honors before Hiro became the first one gone for good.
Jeff T used one rebuy and still could not make the money line.
Wild Bill got Nitro-sized sympathy points for surviving the middle and still missing the podium.
This one never became a circus even with the extra entries.
quickHitsRight: A.I. Dave made the final duel but could not survive the closer.
Rob F built a strong second act by busting Brad R twice and Jeff T once before getting cut down in third.
Li-Fo stayed in the fight long enough to matter, then got swallowed by the closing run.
Gunga Galunga felt less like a sprint and more like a table waiting for someone to take command.
weeks.tldr: BostnMike did not just win Gunga Galunga — he sealed it himself. In a rebuy-heavy June game, the ending belonged to one closer and nobody else.

weeks.title: TWTW: There Will Be Blood - 9 Max
weeks.dek: The league came back from hiatus exactly the way a poker league should: a little delayed, a little weird, and with Hiro closing out the opener after Dan K did most of the dirty work.

weeks.mainStoryHtml:
There Will Be Blood turned out to be a pretty honest title for the first game back. The room had been quiet, then suddenly everybody was buying in on one day, Jeff T showed up the next, and the event started with the kind of mild procedural weirdness that makes a comeback feel real.

Dan K did most of the early damage. Wild Bill went through him twice, and BostnMike joined the list before the game got short. That made Dan K feel like the main threat of the opener right up until Hiro removed him and took over the finish. Once that happened, the first game back got its winner line fast: Dan K out, Li-Fo out, Hiro first.

Jeff T was the first bust and also the first one gone for good, the two rebuys kept the little field alive just long enough, and the whole thing ended with a very clean message: the league was back, and it was already up to its old habits.
weeks.roastHtml: BostnMike got first blood in the season opener and still somehow managed to end up as part of Dan K’s highlight package. That is vintage commissioner form: useful in the opening scene, missing by the climax.
numbersThatMatter: 6 buy-ins, 2 rebuys, 8 total entries.
3h 59m of play from 7:08 pm to 11:07 pm.
$240 total pot with only 2 paid spots.
Hiro took the final two knockouts after Dan K had handled most of the earlier bloodshed.
quickHitsLeft: Jeff T bought in the day after everybody else and still got first-bust honors once the cards were live.
Wild Bill used one rebuy and still wound up as a repeat victim for Dan K.
Li-Fo took Jeff T out for good and still made heads-up.
The opener had a tiny field, but it did not play timid.
quickHitsRight: Only two spots paid, which made the late busts feel immediate and final.
BostnMike opened the season with a knockout and closed it with a receipt against him instead.
The runtime stayed under four hours, which made the opener feel clean and easy to digest.
There Will Be Blood was a fitting first chapter: some rust, some rebuys, and a very recognizable finish.
weeks.tldr: Hiro won the first game back by taking the final two knockouts after Dan K handled most of the early damage. The hiatus ended, and the receipts started immediately.
